topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The wrought-iron gate groaned a protest as Leo pushed it open, the sound echoing unnervingly in the sudden silence of the overgrown drive. He kept his eyes fixed on the peeling paint of the porch, his knuckles white where he gripped the handle of the antique doorknob. Every shadow seemed to shift, elongating into something watchful. The air inside the house was thick, smelling of dust and forgotten lavender, and it made the fine hairs on his arms prickle. He took a shallow breath, the movement catching in his chest. "Well," he managed, his voice sounding thin and reedy, "this is… a lot." He took a tentative step onto the warped wooden floorboards, his gaze darting from the dark corners to the imposing, silent furniture.

[story 2]

Maya traced the intricate carvings on the mantelpiece, the coolness of the marble seeping through the thin fabric of her dress. A tremor ran through her hands, making the silver locket she wore swing slightly. She kept glancing toward the tall, arched windows, half-expecting movement in the dense foliage beyond the glass. The silence of the old manor pressed in on her ears, amplifying the frantic drumming of her own pulse. "It feels wrong," she whispered to the empty room, the words barely audible above the creak of the settling foundation. She turned abruptly, her posture rigid, and ran a hand over the rough texture of a tapestry, seeking some anchor in the unsettling stillness.

[story 3]

He paced the length of the grand hall three times before stopping abruptly by the staircase, his breathing quick and shallow. The floorboards beneath his worn boots seemed to complain with every heavy tread. A strange tension coiled tight in his stomach, a familiar, unwelcome knot that tightened with each passing minute. He stared at the portrait hanging above the fireplace—the painted eyes seemed to follow him. "We should just leave," he muttered, his tone sharp, laced with a brittle impatience. He didn't look at his sister, focusing instead on the distant, flickering light filtering through the grimy windowpanes, as if seeking an escape route in the dimness.
